differentials.name: Rate of hemorrhage ◊  If bleeding not detectable by RBC scintigraphy, it will not be detectable by angiography!
differentials.raw: Rate of hemorrhage ◊  If bleeding not detectable by RBC scintigraphy, it will not be detectable by angiography!

differentials.name: RBC scan detects bleeding as low as 0.1 mL/min
differentials.raw: RBC scan detects bleeding as low as 0.1 mL/min

differentials.name: Catheter angiography requires bleeding rates of ≥ 0.5 mL/min:
differentials.raw: Catheter angiography requires bleeding rates of ≥ 0.5 mL/min:

differentials.name: Continuous versus intermittent bleeding: most GI hemorrhages are intermittent
differentials.raw: Continuous versus intermittent bleeding: most GI hemorrhages are intermittent

differentials.name: Site of hemorrhage in reference to specific vascular territory: eg, celiac, superior mesenteric, inferior mesenteric artery
differentials.raw: Site of hemorrhage in reference to specific vascular territory: eg, celiac, superior mesenteric, inferior mesenteric artery

differentials.name: Characteristics of radionuclide agent 99mTc-Labeled Autologous RBCs (In Vivtro Labeling Preferred) ◊  Generally preferred and accepted most sensitive imaging method for lower GI bleeding ◊  Serves to triage patients for angiography as a negative exam predicts a negative arteriogram Indications:   acute / intermittent arterial or venous bleeding; NOT useful in occult bleeding
differentials.raw: Characteristics of radionuclide agent 99mTc-Labeled Autologous RBCs (In Vivtro Labeling Preferred) ◊  Generally preferred and accepted most sensitive imaging method for lower GI bleeding ◊  Serves to triage patients for angiography as a negative exam predicts a negative arteriogram Indications:   acute / intermittent arterial or venous bleeding; NOT useful in occult bleeding

context: Detection depends on: 63% (39%) sensitive for upper (lower) GI bleed ◊  Only 50% of angiograms will be positive after a positive scintigram! ◊  A positive scintigram increases likelihood of a positive angiogram from 22% to 53%! ◊  A hemodynamically unstable patient with systolic pressures < 100 mmHg should go to angiography! Pharmacokinetics: »  remains in vascular system for a prolonged period »  liver + spleen activity are low allowing detection of upper GI tract hemorrhage »  low target-to-background ratio (high activity in great vessels, liver, spleen, kidneys, stomach, colon; probably rel
